source: Paper Assignments table provided by user 2026-04-22; cross-checked against banking_corp_finance_memory_sheet_v2.pdf

papers.title: Banks as Liquidity Providers: An Explanation for the Coexistence of Lending and Deposit-Taking
papers.main_point: Deposits and credit-line draws aren't perfectly correlated, so the same liquidity buffer serves both sides.

papers.title: Banks' Advantage in Hedging Liquidity Risk: Theory and Evidence from the Commercial Paper Market
papers.main_point: When market liquidity dries up, deposits flow into banks while firms draw credit lines — a natural hedge.

papers.title: Are Banks Really Special? New Evidence from the FDIC-Induced Failure of Healthy Banks
papers.main_point: Even healthy-bank failures hurt local real activity — relationships matter beyond borrower quality.

papers.title: Bank Liquidity Creation
papers.main_point: Builds a broad liquidity-creation measure; banks fund illiquid assets with liquid liabilities; capital and size matter.

papers.title: What Do a Million Observations on Banks Say about the Transmission of Monetary Policy?
papers.main_point: Tightening cuts lending more at small, illiquid banks — supporting the bank balance-sheet channel.

papers.title: Credit Supply and Monetary Policy: Identifying the Bank Balance-Sheet Channel with Loan Applications
papers.main_point: Spanish loan-application data separates supply from demand; tight policy cuts supply especially at weaker banks.

papers.title: The Deposits Channel of Monetary Policy
papers.main_point: Banks with market power widen deposit spreads when rates rise; deposits flee; lending contracts — a deposits channel.

papers.title: The Effects of Quantitative Easing on Bank Lending Behavior
papers.main_point: QE raised MBS holdings' value and recapitalized exposed banks, which then expanded lending more.

papers.title: The Finance-Growth Nexus: Evidence from Bank Branch Deregulation
papers.main_point: Branch deregulation raised state growth mainly via lending quality, not volume.

papers.title: Collateral Damage: Effects of the Japanese Bank Crisis on Real Activity in the United States
papers.main_point: Japanese-bank distress transmitted to U.S. borrowers via affiliates — bank shocks spill across borders.

papers.title: Tracing the Impact of Bank Liquidity Shocks: Evidence from an Emerging Market
papers.main_point: Same-firm-different-bank design isolates supply shocks; liquidity hits to a bank contract its lending.

papers.title: Exports and Financial Shocks
papers.main_point: Trade-finance-bank health shocks cut exports relative to domestic sales, explaining crisis export collapses.

papers.title: Securitized Banking and the Run on Repo
papers.main_point: 2007-08 was a modern bank run in the repo market against securitized short-term funding.

papers.title: Did Securitization Lead to Lax Screening? Evidence from Subprime Loans
papers.main_point: FICO-620 securitization cutoff shows lenders screened less when loans were securitized — defaults jumped.

papers.title: The Consequences of Mortgage Credit Expansion: Evidence from the US Mortgage Default Crisis
papers.main_point: Credit supply grew fastest in low-productivity ZIPs with weak fundamentals; this supply shift drove the default crisis.

papers.title: Bank Lending During the Financial Crisis of 2008
papers.main_point: Syndicated lending fell sharply in 2008; banks with weak deposits and Lehman exposure cut more.

papers.title: Understanding Bank Runs: The Importance of Depositor-Bank Relationships and Networks
papers.main_point: Depositor-level run data: longer relationships and deposit insurance reduce run probability; social networks transmit panics.

papers.title: Deposit Competition and Financial Fragility: Evidence from the US Banking Sector
papers.main_point: Structural model: insured deposits are sticky; uninsured run on bad news; fragility comes from deposit-market competition.

papers.title: Runs on Money Market Mutual Funds
papers.main_point: 2008 MMMF run: institutional investors ran faster than retail; first-mover advantage amplified panic.

papers.title: Monetary Tightening and U.S. Bank Fragility in 2023
papers.main_point: 2022-23 rate hikes cut US bank asset values ~10%; many banks insolvent on mark-to-market if uninsured depositors run — SVB mechanics.

papers.title: Who Manages Risk? An Empirical Examination of Risk Management Practices in the Gold Mining Industry
papers.main_point: Gold miners' hedging is driven more by managerial risk preferences and compensation than by shareholder-value theories.

papers.title: Why Firms Use Currency Derivatives
papers.main_point: Firms with greater growth opportunities and tighter financial constraints are more likely to use FX derivatives — underinvestment-motive evidence.

papers.title: Do Firms Hedge in Response to Tax Incentives?
papers.main_point: Hedging reflects debt capacity rather than a convex tax function; firms hedge to increase debt and its tax shield.

papers.title: Financing Policy, Basis Risk, and Corporate Hedging: Evidence from Oil and Gas Producers
papers.main_point: Oil-and-gas hedging rises with leverage and economies-of-scale in contract costs; basis risk shapes which instruments are used.

papers.title: The Use of Foreign Currency Derivatives and Firm Market Value
papers.main_point: FX hedgers trade at ~4.87% Tobin-Q premium over non-hedgers — a positive hedging-premium result.

papers.title: Firm Value and Hedging: Evidence from U.S. Oil and Gas Producers
papers.main_point: In oil-and-gas producers, hedging has no significant effect on firm value after controlling for exposure — challenges the hedging-premium.

papers.title: Does Hedging Affect Firm Value? Evidence from a Natural Experiment
papers.main_point: Using shale-boom instrument for hedging ability, hedging raises firm value in financially constrained producers.

papers.title: Limits to Arbitrage and Hedging: Evidence from Commodity Markets
papers.main_point: When producers are financially constrained, their hedging demand drives commodity risk premia — hedging pressure hypothesis evidence.

papers.title: Determinants of Trader Profits in Commodity Futures Markets
papers.main_point: Trader-level futures data: profits accrue to large commercial hedgers and sophisticated speculators; risk premia compensate limited arbitrage capital.

papers.title: CEO Compensation and Corporate Risk: Evidence from a Natural Experiment
papers.main_point: After a mandated left-tail-risk shock (liability rule), firms cut vega; CEO risk-taking incentives drop in response to downside risk.

papers.title: The Causal Effect of Option Pay on Corporate Risk Management
papers.main_point: FAS 123R as a shock to option pay: lower option pay raises hedging, consistent with convex pay reducing hedging incentives.

papers.title: Are Firms Successful at Selective Hedging?
papers.main_point: Evidence that managers attempt selective/timing hedges but generate little added value — hedging looks closer to rule-of-thumb than market-timing skill.

papers.title: Taking a View: Corporate Speculation, Governance, and Compensation
papers.main_point: Firms speculating with derivatives have stronger governance and tie compensation to outcomes — speculation is disciplined where risk is understood.

papers.title: Capital Expenditures, Financial Constraints, and the Use of Options
papers.main_point: Gold mines with tighter financing constraints use options more than forwards; derivatives choice reflects insurance of capex plans.

papers.title: Offshore Activities and Financial vs Operational Hedging
papers.main_point: Text-based measure of offshore activities: operational hedging via foreign operations substitutes for financial derivatives.

papers.title: Why Do U.S. Firms Hold So Much More Cash Than They Used To?
papers.main_point: Rising cash ratios from 10% to 23% reflect precautionary motives: R&D intensity, higher idiosyncratic risk, weaker cash-flow predictability.

papers.title: How Valuable is Financial Flexibility When Revenue Stops? Evidence from the COVID-19 Crisis
papers.main_point: In the COVID revenue shock, financially flexible firms (low leverage, high cash) did dramatically better on returns — flexibility has option value.

papers.title: Environmental Risk Management and the Cost of Capital
papers.main_point: Better environmental risk management is associated with lower cost of equity and higher leverage — linking ERM to firm value.

papers.title: The Price of Sin: The Effects of Social Norms on Markets
papers.main_point: Sin stocks (alcohol, tobacco, gaming) earn higher returns and have less analyst coverage — norm-constrained investors avoid them.

papers.title: Environmental Externalities and Cost of Capital
papers.main_point: Firms with greater environmental concerns face higher cost of equity/debt, fewer institutional holders, and smaller lender syndicates.

papers.title: Corporate Environmental Policy and Shareholder Value: Following the Smart Money
papers.main_point: Institutional investors prefer firms with lower environmental risk and higher green credentials — environmental policy moves shareholder value.

papers.title: Social Capital, Trust, and Firm Performance: The Value of CSR during the Financial Crisis
papers.main_point: High-CSR firms outperformed by 4-7% in the 2008-09 crisis — social capital acts as insurance when trust collapses.

papers.title: The Consequences of Managerial Indiscretions: Sex, Lies, and Firm Value
papers.main_point: Managerial personal indiscretions predict worse firm outcomes and governance failures — 'social' risk has real pricing implications.

papers.title: Disaster on the Horizon: The Price Effect of Sea Level Rise
papers.main_point: Properties exposed to sea-level rise trade at a ~7% discount — climate risk is priced in coastal real estate.

papers.title: Nature or Nurture? The Source of Climate Risk Beliefs
papers.main_point: Climate beliefs embedded in prices track political identity and local exposure — beliefs (not just exposure) drive the SLR discount.

papers.title: Is the Risk of Sea Level Rise Capitalized in Residential Real Estate?
papers.main_point: Accounting for land subsidence, SLR risk has small-to-zero capitalization in coastal home prices — beliefs/time horizons matter.

papers.title: Temperature Shocks and Establishment Sales
papers.main_point: Extreme heat reduces establishment sales in exposed industries — physical climate damages already showing in firm-level data.

papers.title: The Importance of Climate Risks for Institutional Investors
papers.main_point: Survey of institutional investors: climate risks are financially material; risk-management motive dominates ethical motive.

papers.title: Do Institutional Investors Drive Corporate Social Responsibility?
papers.main_point: Cross-country evidence that institutional ownership raises firm CSR scores — foreign institutions especially push social performance.

papers.title: Corporate Climate Risk: Measurements and Responses
papers.main_point: Build firm-level climate-risk measures from 10-K text; firms respond by raising cash, cutting investment, and changing capital structure.

papers.title: Sustainability or Greenwashing? Evidence from the Asset Market for Industrial Pollution
papers.main_point: Divestments of polluting assets often shift emissions to less-regulated buyers — emission reductions are partly greenwashing.

papers.title: Neglected No More: Housing Markets, Mortgage Lending, and Sea Level Rise
papers.main_point: SLR risk is being priced into coastal housing and mortgage markets as information spreads — late-adjustment evidence contrary to Murfin-Spiegel.

papers.title: Firm Responses to Climate Regulation
papers.main_point: Firms respond to tighter climate regulation by reallocating emissions across facilities and jurisdictions more than by cutting total emissions.

papers.title: Risk Management, Firm Reputation, and the Impact of Successful Cyberattacks on Target Firms
papers.main_point: Successful cyberattacks reduce target firm value, harm reputation with customers, and change firms' subsequent risk-management investments.

papers.title: Hacking Corporate Reputations
papers.main_point: Cyberattacks affect firm reputation and stakeholder trust beyond direct financial costs — reputational damage is a distinct risk channel.
